coredata.dc:title: The Improved Performance of porous Sn-Ni Alloy as Anode Materials for Lithium- Ion Battery prepared by Electrochemical Dissolution Treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 The Authors. Published by ESG. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by ESG. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Chunhui
Qi, Gongwei
Li, Yeping
Guo, Jing
Wang, Xin
Kong, Delong
Wang, Hongjun
Zhang, Shuyong
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve the specific capacity and rate capability of the electrodeposited Sn-Ni alloy, a post electrochemical dissolution treatment was introduced to prepare porous Sn-Ni alloy. Experimental results suggest that composition of the alloys can be controlled by changing the electroplating condition evidenced by atomic absorption spectrophotometer. Porous structure formed and Sn phase lost in electrochemical dissolution progress evidenced by scanning electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. Porous Sn-Ni alloys deliver a higher specific capacity than as-deposited Sn-Ni alloys. The specific capacity of the best one delivers ca. 540 mAhg-1 after 50 cycles. This improvement is attributing to reduction of Sn content and formation of nanopore in the alloy matrix after the post electrochemical dissolution treatment. The post electrochemical dissolution treatment is an effective way to improve the performance of Sn-Ni alloy as the anode material for lithium-ion battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ion battery
anode materials
Sn-Ni alloy
electrochemical dissolution
porous structure

National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: ACKNOWLEDGEMENT This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Project No. ZR2009BM012) and the 973 Project of China (No. 2011CB935901).

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: The Improved Performance of porous Sn-Ni Alloy as Anode Materials for Lithium- Ion Battery prepared by Electrochemical Dissolution Treatment Chunhui Tan, 1 Gongwei Qi, 1 Yeping Li, 1 Jing Guo, 1 Xin Wang, 1 Delong Kong, 2 Hongjun Wang, 2 Shuyong Zhang, 1 syzhang@sdu.edu.cn 1 Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, China, 250100 Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Shandong University Jinan 250100 China 1 Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, China, 250100 2 Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd. Qufu, China, 273100 Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd Qufu 273100 China 2 Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd. Qufu, China, 273100 In order to improve the specific capacity and rate capability of the electrodeposited Sn-Ni alloy, a post electrochemical dissolution treatment was introduced to prepare porous Sn-Ni alloy. Experimental results suggest that composition of the alloys can be controlled by changing the electroplating condition evidenced by atomic absorption spectrophotometer. Porous structure formed and Sn phase lost in electrochemical dissolution progress evidenced by scanning electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. Porous Sn-Ni alloys deliver a higher specific capacity than as-deposited Sn-Ni alloys. The specific capacity of the best one delivers ca. 540 mAhg-1 after 50 cycles. This improvement is attributing to reduction of Sn content and formation of nanopore in the alloy matrix after the post electrochemical dissolution treatment. The post electrochemical dissolution treatment is an effective way to improve the performance of Sn-Ni alloy as the anode material for lithium-ion battery.Keywords Lithium ion battery anode materials Sn-Ni alloy electrochemical dissolution porous structure References 1. J. Tarascon, M. Armand, Nature 414: (2001) 359-J. Tarascon and M. Armand, Nature, 414 (2001) 359. 2. M. Winter, J. Besenhard, Electrochim. Acta 45: (1999) 31-M. Winter and J. Besenhard, Electrochim. Acta, 45 (1999) 31. 3. I. Courtney, J. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc. 144: (1997) 2045-I. Courtney and J. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc., 144 (1997) 2045. 4. D. Jiang, X. Ma, Y. Fu, J. Appl. Electrochem. 42: (2012) 555-559 D. Jiang, X. Ma and Y. Fu, J. Appl. Electrochem., 42 (2012) 555â€“559. 5. H. Jung, E. Kim, Y. Park, H. Shin, Nickelâ€“tin foam with nanostructured walls for rechargeable lithium battery J. Power Sources 196: (2011) 5122-H. Jung, E. Kim, Y. Park and H. Shin, Nickelâ€“tin foam with nanostructured walls for rechargeable lithium battery, J. Power Sources, 196 (2011) 5122. 6. M. Lu, Y. Tian, Y. Li, W. Li, X. Zheng, B. Huang, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci. 7: (2012) 760-M. Lu, Y. Tian, Y. Li, W. Li, X. Zheng and B. Huang, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci., 7 (2012) 760. 7. H. Guo, S. Zhao, H. Zhao, Y. Chen, Electrochim. Acta 54: (2009) 4040-H. Guo, S. Zhao, H. Zhao and Y. Chen, Electrochim. Acta, 54 (2009) 4040. 8. Y. Wang, L. Huang, Y. Chang, F. Ke, J. Li, S. Sun, Electrochem. Commun. 12: (2010) 1226-Y. Wang, L. Huang, Y. Chang, F. Ke, J. Li and S. Sun, Electrochem. Commun., 12 (2010) 1226. 9. J. Hassoun, S. Panero, P. Simon, P. Taberna, B. Scrosati, Adv. Mater. 19: (2007) 1632-J. Hassoun, S. Panero, P. Simon, P. Taberna and B. Scrosati, Adv. Mater., 19 (2007) 1632. 10. Z. Du, S. Zhang, Y. Xing, X. Wu, J. Power Sources 196: (2011) 9780-Z. Du, S. Zhang, Y. Xing and X. Wu, J. Power Sources, 196 (2011) 9780. 11. M. Kotobuki, N. Okada, K. Kanamura, Chem. Commun. 47: (2011) 6144-M. Kotobuki, N. Okada and K. Kanamura, Chem. Commun., 47 (2011) 6144. 12. F. Ke, L. Huang, H. Jiang, H. Wei, F. Yang, S. Sun, Electrochem. Commun. 9: (2007) 228-F. Ke, L. Huang, H. Jiang, H. Wei, F. Yang and S. Sun, Electrochem. Commun., 9 (2007) 228. 13. L. Huang, H. Wei, F. Ke, X. Fan, J. Li, S. Sun, Electrochim. Acta 54: (2009) 2693-L. Huang, H. Wei, F. Ke, X. Fan, J. Li and S. Sun, Electrochim. Acta, 54 (2009) 2693. 14. T. Watanabe, T. Hirose, K. Arai, M. Chikazawa, J. Jpn. Inst. Met. 63: (1999) 496-T. Watanabe, T. Hirose, K. Arai and M. Chikazawa, J. Jpn. Inst. Met., 63 (1999) 496. 15. H. Mukaibo, T. Sumi, T. Yokoshima, T. Momma, T. Osaka, Electrochem. Solid-State Lett. 6: (2003) A218-H. Mukaibo, T. Sumi, T. Yokoshima, T. Momma and T. Osaka, Electrochem. Solid-State Lett., 6 (2003) A218. 16. C. Ionica-Bousquet, P. Lippens, L. Aldon, J. Olivier-Fourcade, J. Jumas, J. Appl. Electrochem. 18: (2006) 6442-C. Ionica-Bousquet, P. Lippens, L. Aldon, J. Olivier-Fourcade and J. Jumas, J. Appl. Electrochem., 18 (2006) 6442. 17. P. Ferguson, R. Dunlap, J. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc. 157: (2010) A326-P. Ferguson, R. Dunlap and J. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc., 157 (2010) A326. handong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd Qufu 273100 China 2 Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd. Qufu, China, 273100 In order to improve the specific capacity and rate capability of the electrodeposited Sn-Ni alloy, a post electrochemical dissolution treatment was introduced to prepare porous Sn-Ni alloy. Experimental results suggest that composition of the alloys can be controlled by changing the electroplating condition evidenced by atomic absorption spectrophotometer. Porous structure formed and Sn phase lost in electrochemical dissolution progress evidenced by scanning electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. Porous Sn-Ni alloys deliver a higher specific capacity than as-deposited Sn-Ni alloys. The specific capacity of the best one delivers ca. 540 mAhg-1 after 50 cycles. This improvement is attributing to reduction of Sn content and formation of nanopore in the alloy matrix after the post electrochemical dissolution treatment. The post electrochemical dissolution treatment is an effective way to improve the performance of Sn-Ni alloy as the anode material for lithium-ion battery.Keywords Lithium ion battery anode materials Sn-Ni alloy electrochemical dissolution porous structure References 1. J. Tarascon, M. Armand, Nature 414: (2001) 359-J. Tarascon and M. Armand, Nature, 414 (2001) 359. 2. M. Winter, J. Besenhard, Electrochim. Acta 45: (1999) 31-M. Winter and J. Besenhard, Electrochim. simple-head.ce:title: The Improved Performance of porous Sn-Ni Alloy as Anode Materials for Lithium- Ion Battery prepared by Electrochemical Dissolution Treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, China, 250100

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd. Qufu, China, 273100

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2 Shandong Sacred Sun Power Co. Ltd. Qufu, China, 273100

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve the specific capacity and rate capability of the electrodeposited Sn-Ni alloy, a post electrochemical dissolution treatment was introduced to prepare porous Sn-Ni alloy. Experimental results suggest that composition of the alloys can be controlled by changing the electroplating condition evidenced by atomic absorption spectrophotometer. Porous structure formed and Sn phase lost in electrochemical dissolution progress evidenced by scanning electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. Porous Sn-Ni alloys deliver a higher specific capacity than as-deposited Sn-Ni alloys. The specific capacity of the best one delivers ca. 540 mAhg-1 after 50 cycles. This improvement is attributing to reduction of Sn content and formation of nanopore in the alloy matrix after the post electrochemical dissolution treatment. The post electrochemical dissolution treatment is an effective way to improve the performance of Sn-Ni alloy as the anode material for lithium-ion battery.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tarascon and M. Armand, Nature, 414 (2001) 359.
M. Winter and J. Besenhard, Electrochim. Acta, 45 (1999) 31.
I. Courtney and J. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc., 144 (1997) 2045.
D. Jiang, X. Ma and Y. Fu, J. Appl. Electrochem., 42 (2012) 555–559.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nickel–tin foam with nanostructured walls for rechargeable lithium battery
